slides.title: Mastering Bash Scripting
slides.subtitle:
The Universal Language of Automation
From Command Line to Production Systems

slides.name: Aravind Govindhasamy
slides.title: Software Developer
slides.experience: Specialist in .NET, Go, and Modern Cloud Architecture
slides.oss_experience: Open Source Contributor & Technical Content Creator

topics.title: 1. Introduction: The Power of the Shell

box.content: The Shell as an interface, not just a command prompt

A shell is a computer program which exposes an operating system's services to a human user or other program. In general, operating system shells use either a command-line interface (CLI) or a graphical user interface (GUI), depending on a computer's role and particular operation.

list.text: Interpreter: It reads your commands and tells the OS what to do.
Environment: It provides tools (variables, history, aliases) to make work easier.
Programming Language: It allows you to wrap sequence of commands into a script.

box.content: While there are many shells today, they mostly derive from a few core ancestors in the Unix ecosystem.

list.text: bash (Bourne Again Shell): The GNU project's enhancement. The standard on Linux.
zsh (Z Shell): Highly customizable, default on macOS.
fish (Friendly Interactive Shell): Modern, focus on UX.

box.content: The Shebang is the first line of a script. It tells the operating system which interpreter should be used to execute the code. Without it, the system might try to run your script using a different (and potentially incompatible) shell.
Managing data and preventing common errors
Variables in Bash are simple. No spaces are allowed around the equals sign! By convention, environment variables are UPPERCASE, and local script variables are lowercase.
box.code:
# Assignment (NO SPACES!)
name="Antigravity"
version=1.0

# Usage (Prefix with $)
echo "Tool: $name v$version"

box.content: You can capture the output of a command and store it in a variable using the $(...) syntax. This is the heart of automation logic.
box.code:
current_dir=$(pwd)
today=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)

echo "Location: $current_dir"
echo "Date: $today"

box.content: Understanding the difference between single and double quotes is CRITICAL in Bash. It's the #1 source of beginner bugs.
box.code:
name="Bude"

# Double Quotes: Variable INTERPOLATION
echo "Hello $name" # Hello Bude

# Single Quotes: LITERAL interpretation
echo 'Hello $name' # Hello $name

box.content: The 'Elite' rule of Bash: **Always wrap variable usages in double quotes.** This prevents the shell from breaking your variable into multiple arguments if it contains spaces.
box.code:
file="Daily Report.txt"

# WRONG: Looks for 'Daily' and 'Report.txt'
rm $file 

# CORRECT: Treats as one file
rm "$file"

box.content: Success, Failure, and Decisions
Logic in Bash usually relies on the test command or its alias [. Modern Bash scripts should prefer the double bracket [[ for better performance and safety.
box.code:
# String comparison
if [[ "$env" == "prod" ]]; then
    echo "Careful!"
fi

# Numerical comparison
if [[ $lines -gt 100 ]]; then
    echo "File is large"
fi

box.content: Every Unix command returns an Exit Status Code (0 to 255) to the shell. This is stored in the special variable $?.

list.text: Non-Zero: Failure. Specific numbers can indicate specific errors.

box.code:
ls /non-existent-dir
echo $? # Prints 2 (or other failure code)

box.content: You can combine commands using && (and) and || (or) based on their exit codes. This allows for very concise 'one-liner' automation.
box.code:
# Run second command ONLY if first succeeds
mkdir logs && touch logs/app.log

# Run second command ONLY if first FAILS
ls data.csv || echo "Data file missing!"

box.content: Mastering streams, redirection, and pipes
Every Unix process has three standard communication channels (file descriptors). Understanding these is key to redirecting errors and capturing output correctly.
Redirection allows you to send output to a file or read input from a file instead of the terminal.
box.code:
# Send output to file (OVERWRITE)
echo "Log started" > app.log

# Send output to file (APPEND)
echo "Update" >> app.log

# Redirect stderr to a separate file
./myscript.sh 2> errors.log

# Redirect both stdout and stderr to the same file
./myscript.sh &> combined.log

box.content: Pipes allow you to connect the stdout of one command directly to the stdin of another. This is the cornerstone of the Unix philosophy: 'Write programs that do one thing and do it well. Write programs to work together.'
box.code:
# Count occurrences of a word in a file
cat access.log | grep "ERROR" | wc -l

# Find top 5 largest files
du -ah | sort -rh | head -n 5

box.content: Here-docs allow you to pass multi-line blocks of text into a command. This is incredibly useful for generating configuration files or SQL scripts from within a Bash script.
box.code:
cat <<EOF > config.yaml
server:
  port: 8080
  env: "$ENV_NAME"
EOF

box.content: Loops, Branching, and Automation
The for loop is used to iterate over a list of items, such as filenames, numbers, or words.
box.code:
# Iterate over files
for file in *.txt; do
    echo "Processing $file"
done

# Numerical range
for i in {1..5}; do
    echo "Round $i"
done

box.content: The while loop continues to execute as long as a condition is true. It's often used for reading files line by line.
box.code:
while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "Line: $line"
done < input.txt

box.content: When you have multiple branches based on a single variable, the case statement is much cleaner and more efficient than a long if/elif chain.
box.code:
case "$OS" in
    "Linux")
        echo "Deploying to Linux"
        ;;
    "FreeBSD" | "OpenBSD")
        echo "Deploying to BSD"
        ;;
    *)
        echo "Unsupported OS"
        exit 1
        ;;
esac

box.content: Encapsulation and Reusability in the Shell
Functions in Bash are a way to group commands. Unlike most languages, you don't declare parameters in the function signature; instead, they are accessed via positional parameters ($1, $2, etc.).
By default, all variables in Bash are **global**. To prevent functions from accidentally overwriting variables in the rest of your script, you MUST use the local keyword.
box.code:
name="Global Antigravity"

update_name() {
    local name="Local Bude"
    echo "Inside: $name"
}

update_name
echo "Outside: $name" # Still 'Global Antigravity'!

box.content: Bash functions don't 'return' objects. They can only return an exit status (0-255). To 'return' data, the common pattern is to echo the result and capture it using command substitution.
Data structures and powerful string expansion
Bash supports one-dimensional indexed arrays. They are useful for storing lists of items like configuration values or lists of files for processing.
box.code:
# Definition
servers=("web01" "web02" "db01")

# Access specific element
echo "First: ${servers[0]}"

# Iterate over all elements
for server in "${servers[@]}"; do
    echo "Checking $server..."
done

box.content: Associative arrays allow you to use strings as keys (like a hash map or dictionary). You must explicitly declare them using declare -A.
box.code:
declare -A config

config[port]=8080
config[env]="prod"

echo "Port is ${config[port]}"

box.content: Bash has a built-in 'mini-language' for manipulating variables during expansion. This is often faster and cleaner than piping to sed or awk.
box.code:
file="backup_2024.tar.gz"

# Substring replacement
echo "${file/backup/archive}" # archive_2024.tar.gz

# Strip suffix
echo "${file%.*}" # backup_2024.tar

# Default values
echo "User: ${DB_USER:-admin}"

box.content: Process substitution allows you to treat the output of a command like a file. This is incredibly useful for commands that expect a filename but you want to provide dynamic output.
box.code:
# Compare output of two commands directly
diff <(ls dir_a) <(ls dir_b)

box.content: Writing production-grade Bash scripts
Most Bash scripts fail silently or continue after errors. The 'Strict Mode' is a set of flags that turn Bash into a much more predictable and safer environment.
box.code:
# The 'Elite' Header
set -euo pipefail

/* 
 -e: Exit on error
 -u: Exit on unset variables
 -o pipefail: Pipeline fails if any command fails
*/

box.content: Scripts often create temporary files or locks. The trap command allows you to specify cleanup logic that runs when the script exits, even if it crashes or is interrupted.
Never assume a directory exists or a command succeeded. Always verify your environment before taking destructive actions.
box.code:
# Check if directory exists before entering
cd "$TARGET_DIR" || { echo "Failed to cd"; exit 1; }

# Check if variable is set
: "${REQUIRED_VAR?Error: REQUIRED_VAR is not set}"

box.content: When things go wrong, Bash provides built-in tools to trace execution. You can enable them globally or for specific blocks of code.
box.code:
# Trace every command (print to stderr)
set -x 

# Add line numbers and script name to trace
export PS4='+(${BASH_SOURCE}:${LINENO}): ${FUNCNAME[0]:+${FUNCNAME[0]}(): }'

# Stop tracing
set +x

box.content: Solved problems and design architecture
Instead of guessing positional parameters, use getopts to provide a professional, Unix-standard command line interface for your scripts.
To prevent multiple instances of a cron job or script from running simultaneously, use a lock file (often with flock).
box.code:
# Wait for lock, then run command
( flock -x 200 || exit 1
  # ... critical section ...
) 200>/var/lock/mylockfile

box.content: In automation, logs are your only window into the past. Always include timestamps, levels, and script metadata.
Tools that help you write better scripts
ShellCheck is a static analysis tool that automatically finds bugs and non-standard practices in your shell scripts. It is the absolute 'Gold Standard' for shell scripting quality.

list.text: Bug detection: Catches quoting issues and logic errors.
Style: Suggests modern alternatives for legacy syntax.

box.content: Sometimes Bash isn't enough. Many modern tools provide 'shell-like' simplicity with modern language features.

list.text: Rust tools: bat, fd, rg (Modern replacements for cat, find, grep).

box.content: Bash is the 'glue' of the internet. Mastering it means mastering the ability to automate almost any task on almost any system.

list.text: The foundation of DevOps and CI/CD.

box.content: Avoiding common mistakes and 'Gotchas'
Bash splits unquoted variables into multiple arguments based on whitespace. This can lead to disastrous results when handling filenames with spaces.
box.code:
files="Daily Report.pdf"

# WRONG: rm thinks you mean two files
rm $files 

# CORRECT: Treat as one string
rm "$files"

box.content: If you use wildcards like * or ? in unquoted variables, Bash will expand them to match files in the current directory, which can be a massive security risk.
box.code:
search_term="*"

# WRONG: if unquoted, this command might expand to everything!
echo $search_term

box.content: The single bracket [ is an external command with strict rules. The double bracket [[ is a shell keyword that handles empty variables and regex much better.
box.code:
# WRONG: fails if name is empty
if [ $name = "Bude" ]; then ...

# CORRECT: safe and modern
if [[ $name == "Bude" ]]; then ...

box.content: Real-world production scripts
A robust script to back up a database, compress it, and rotate old logs.
box.code:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

BACKUP_DIR="/mnt/backups/db"
DATE=$(date +%Y%m%d)

mysqldump my_app > "$BACKUP_DIR/db_$DATE.sql"
gzip "$BACKUP_DIR/db_$DATE.sql"

# Delete backups older than 7 days
find "$BACKUP_DIR" -name "*.gz" -mtime +7 -delete

box.content: Automating the resizing of all images in a directory using a loop and the convert tool.
box.code:
mkdir -p thumbnails

for img in *.jpg; do
    echo "Processing $img..."
    convert "$img" -resize 200x200 "thumbnails/${img%.*}.thumb.jpg"
done

box.content: Don't build massive scripts at first. Use the Unix philosophy: start with a pipe, then a loop, then a function, then a script.
